title: Face Detection: Finding the Face
concept: Face detection locates human faces in an image and draws bounding boxes, serving as the first step for all facial analysis pipelines.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary difference between semantic segmentation and instance segmentation?

options.text: Instance segmentation distinguishes between separate objects of the same class, while semantic does not.
Semantic segmentation draws rectangular bounding boxes, while instance paints pixel masks.
Semantic segmentation is only used for background scenes, while instance is only for people.

exercises.explain: Correct! Instance segmentation gives each separate object a unique ID, whereas semantic segmentation groups all objects of a class together.

cards.title: Starting the camera
cards.body: When you open your phone camera, you immediately see yellow squares popping up around your friends' faces. Before the phone can adjust the focus or apply a filter, it must solve a fundamental challenge: finding where the faces are in the frame.

cards.title: What is Face Detection?
cards.body: Face Detection is the task of identifying the presence and location of human faces in an image or video. It outputs bounding box coordinates (x, y, width, height) indicating where each face is located.

cards.title: Detecting vs. Naming
cards.body: Think of face detection like scanning a crowd and counting how many human heads you see. You aren't trying to recognize who they are; you're just pointing your finger and saying, 'There is a face here, and another one there!'

cards.title: Camera auto-focus
cards.body: Modern DSLR and smartphone cameras use face detection to locate faces in milliseconds, adjusting the lens focus and exposure levels on the detected boxes to ensure people look sharp and clear.

cards.title: Detection is not identification
cards.body: A common myth is that face detection knows who you are. It doesn't! A detector only classifies a region as a 'human face' or 'not a face'. It cannot tell the difference between a stranger, a friend, or a celebrity.

cards.title: Detection outputs
cards.body: What a face detector sees vs. ignores:

left.text:
Detected as a face
Outputs bounding box
Used for cropping

right.text:
Ignored by detector
No coordinates output
Saves computing power

exercises.prompt: What is the primary output of a face detection model?

options.text: The name and identity of the person
Bounding box coordinates of detected faces
A 3D mesh model of the skull

exercises.explain: Face detection models locate faces and return their coordinates as rectangular bounding boxes.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Face detection models must know the identity of the person to locate their face.
exercises.explain: False. Face detection simply finds the presence of a face; it does not recognize who the face belongs to.

exercises.sentence: Face detection outputs bounding box ___ indicating where the faces are located.
exercises.explain: The model outputs bounding box coordinates (x, y, width, height) to frame the faces.

exercises.prompt: Which of these tasks is a pure face detection task?

options.text: A phone camera drawing a square around a person's face to focus the lens
A security system unlocking a door by verifying a user's face
An app tag suggesting your friend's name in a photo

exercises.explain: Drawing focus squares around faces is detection; mapping them to names is recognition.

exercises.prompt: You are building a face-blurring app to protect people's privacy in public street views. What type of model should you use and why?

options.text: A face detection model, because you only need to find the location of the faces to blur them, regardless of who they belong to.
A face recognition model, because you must check if the people are in your database first.
An image classification model, because you need to classify if the entire photo contains a street.
A style transfer model, to paint artistic overlays over the faces.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Face detection is the correct choice here because you only need to locate the faces to apply the blur filter. Excellent job! Next up, let's learn how to actually recognize WHO the face belongs to.
